coredata.dc:title: Low temperature synthesis of highly stable phosphate functionalized two color carbon nanodots and their application in cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zi-Qiang
Yang, Li-Yun
Fan, Xiao-Yang
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Mei, Jie
Peng, Wu
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Xiao, Qi
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Phosphate functionalized carbon nanodots (C-dots) with satisfactory quantum yield have been prepared through one-step acidic oxidation of sucrose by H3PO4 at low temperature (60°C). The as-prepared C-dots are monodisperse with narrow size distribution. Besides, two C-dots emitting different colors, namely green and yellow, can be easily separated by collecting the supernatant and precipitate after neutralization and centrifugation. Both C-dots show good photoluminescence properties over a very wide pH range (2.0–13.0). Moreover, they exhibit high tolerance to various external conditions, such as high ionic strength, external cations and UV excitation. The obtained C-dots can be internalized by cells without post surface passivation and they exhibit minimum toxicity as well as favorable biocompatibility. The highly stable, phosphate functionalized and two color C-dots with narrow size distribution are expected to have great potential application in bioimaging and bioanalysis.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism study on the origin of PL
Biocompatibility and application in cell imaging of C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of the phosphate functionalized green C-dots and yellow C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of the green C-dots (a) and yellow C-dots (b) assembled on Cu grid coated with ultrathin amorphous carbon film. (c–f) show the HRTEM image of green C-dots (c and d) and yellow C-dots (e and f). Both C-dots show lattice spacing about 0.34 and 0.21nm. (g) and (h): diameter distribution of green C-dots and yellow C-dots, the black line is the Gaussian fitting curve. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for green C-dots (a) and yellow C-dots (b). FTIR spectra of green C-dots and yellow C-dots (c). 31P NMR spectra of green C-dots and yellow C-dots (d). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorption spectra of C-dots (a). Excitation and emission spectra of the C-dots (b). Excitation dependent emission spectra of the green C-dots (c) and yellow C-dots (d). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The influence of pH values on the PL intensity of both C-dots. (b) Effect of ionic strengths on the PL intensity of both C-dot (ionic strengths were controlled by various concentrations of KCl). (c) Effect of various external cations (30μmol L-1) on the PL intensity of C-dots. (d) Dependence of PL intensity on excitation time for both C-dots under 365nm. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Viability of the HeLa (a) or GES-1 cells (b) after 48h incubation with the green C-dots or yellow C-dots (mean±SD, n =4). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with 200μg mL-1 green C-dots (a–c) or 200μg mL-1 yellow C-dots (e–f) for 18h. (a) and (d) show the bright-field image of HeLa cells. The fluorescence images Hela cells labeled with green C-dots (b) or yellow C-dots (e) with 405nm excitation. Merged images of HeLa cells incubated with green C-dots (c) or yellow C-dots (f). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Low temperature synthesis of highly stable phosphate functionalized two color carbon nanodots and their application in cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphate functionalized carbon nanodots (C-dots) with satisfactory quantum yield have been prepared through one-step acidic oxidation of sucrose by H3PO4 at low temperature (60°C). The as-prepared C-dots are monodisperse with narrow size distribution. Besides, two C-dots emitting different colors, namely green and yellow, can be easily separated by collecting the supernatant and precipitate after neutralization and centrifugation. Both C-dots show good photoluminescence properties over a very wide pH range (2.0–13.0). Moreover, they exhibit high tolerance to various external conditions, such as high ionic strength, external cations and UV excitation. The obtained C-dots can be internalized by cells without post surface passivation and they exhibit minimum toxicity as well as favorable biocompatibility. The highly stable, phosphate functionalized and two color C-dots with narrow size distribution are expected to have great potential application in bioimaging and bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted great attention in fluorescent nanoparticles due to their superior physical and chemical properties. Compared with the conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, C-dots are superior in the aspects of high aqueous solubility, outstanding photoluminescence (PL) properties, favorable biocompatibility, chemical inertness and easy functionalization [1]. As a result, they have emerged as candidates for a variety of practical applications, such as bioimaging [2,3], chemical sensor and biosensor [4–6], drug delivery [7], gene delivery [8], dye sensitizers [9], photocatalysis [10,11], fluorescent ink [12] and mimetics peroxidase [13]. Therefore, it is worthwhile to search for superior synthesis methods.

Up to now, extensive efforts have been devoted to synthesis of C-dots and their synthesis methods can be divided into two types, that is chemical and physical methods [1]. Chemical methods consist of electrochemical [14], thermal treatment [15], hydrothermal or acidic oxidation [16], microwave [17], ultrasonic [18], cage-opening of fullerene [19], and so on. Physical methods include arc discharge [20], laser ablation [21], and plasma treatment [22]. Among these methods, acidic oxidation has been proved to be an effective and convenient way for synthesis of C-dots with sufficient fluorescence quantum yield (QY) without requiring elaborate apparatus.

Recently, with the rapid development of synthesis methods, the synthesis of functionalized or doped C-dots has become another hot topic. Park et al. [23] synthesized sulfur-incorporated C-dots with a very strong long-wavelength absorption band. Chi et al. [24] synthesized polyamine functionalized C-dots with high QY by carbonization of citric acid with branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) in one-step. Zheng et al. [25] synthesized reduced state C-dots by reducing C-dots with sodium borohydride. The QY of the reduced state C-dots increased from 2% to 24% and maximum PL wavelength shifted from 520 to 450nm.

However, to the best of our knowledge, synthesis of phosphate functionalized C-dots emitting green or yellow PL with narrow size distribution has rarely been reported. Pramanik et al. [26] synthesized phosphate functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) by microwave irradiation. The size distribution of the as-prepared CNPs was 3–10nm and they showed a PL at 454.5nm. Unfortunately, the size distribution and PL properties of CNPs were not well enough to apply in bioimaging directly. So they further functionalized them with organic dyes to improve the PL properties of CNPs.

We are interested in developing convenient ways to synthesize phosphate functionalized and multi-color C-dots from environmentally friendly materials and simple apparatus. Unlike Pramanik’s work, we have developed a more convenient and economical way to synthesize two color C-dots by acidic oxidation using simple apparatus at low temperature (60°C). Interestingly, we successfully obtained phosphate functionalized C-dots emitting green or yellow PL without post surface passivation. In addition, the as-prepared C-dots are monodisperse with narrow size distribution and possess satisfactory PL properties. Meanwhile, their stability under various external conditions (different pH, high ionic strengths, external cations and UV excitation) has also been studied. Furthermore, we studied the biocompatibility of the C-dots and explored their potential as promising bioimaging agent.

Moreover, the obtained C-dots have potential application in osteogenic differentiation for that phosphates had been reported to play an important role in mineralized tissue development such as bone formation [27]. Meanwhile, they may also be applied as a good supercapacitor as Lu et al. [28] reported the extraordinary supercapacitive performance of phosphorus-rich microporous carbons prepared by a simple H3PO4 activation.

Sucrose, H3PO4, NaOH and KCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Aladdin reagent Co. (China). All reagents were used as received and aqueous solution was prepared with deionized water (18.2MΩ cm−1, Millipore).

(1) 1g sucrose was dissolved in 4mL deionized water. 20mL H3PO4 was added and the solution was kept at 60°C under vigorously stirring for 4h. The color of the solution gradually changed from transparent colorless to brownish black through orange and red. (2) The mixture was neutralized with NaOH and then centrifuged (6000rpm/min) for 10min to separate the green-emitting C-dots (denoted as green C-dots) containing supernatant from yellow-emitting C-dots (denoted as yellow C-dots) containing oily precipitate. (3) The oily precipitate was dissolved in deionized water. Then the supernatant and dissolved oily precipitate were filtered with a 0.22μm polyethersulfone (PES) membrane to remove the larger carbon nanoparticles. Finally, the obtained C-dots were purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a membrane with 1000 MWCO for 2days.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) electron microscope operating at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg as the exciting source. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of C-dots were recorded Thermo (USA) FTIR spectrophotometer. Phosphorus-31 nuclear magnetic resonance (31P NMR) spectra were obtained on a JEOL ECX (150MHz) instrument and D2O was used as the solvent. Fluorescence analysis was performed on LS-55 fluorophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). UNICO 4802 UV–vis double-beam spectrophotometer was used to measure absorption spectra of C-dots.

The Human epithelial carcinoma (HeLa) and Human Gastric Epithelial cell line (GES-1) was cultured in dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin, and 1% amphotericin at 37°C in 5% CO2. The cells were seeded into 96-well plate at a density of 1×104 per well and allowed to adhere for 12h. Serial dilutions of C-dots with known concentration were added into well. After incubation for 48h, MTT with a concentration of 5mg mL−1 was added into each well, the cells were allowed to grow for 4h until a purple precipitate was visible. The medium was then removed and 150μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added. The clusters were vibrated for 15min to completely dissolve the crystals. Finally, the absorption at 490nm was measured by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA).

HeLa cells were seeded in culture dishes and cultured at 37°C. Before they were added into the culture dishes, the separately prepared aqueous solution of green C-dots or yellow C-dots were filtered with a 0.22μm PES membrane and then diluted with fresh cell culture medium to the specific concentrations (200μg mL−1). After incubation for 18h, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and used for confocal fluorescence imaging. The extent of staining was visualized by Perkin–Elmer Ultraview Vox confocal microscope equipped with a spectral detection system.

The synthesis of C-dots was performed according to the acidic oxidation applying sucrose as the carbon source and H3PO4 as the oxidizing agent which was similar to carbonization of ascorbic acid at 90°C [29] (Fig. 1 ).

On the basis of previous studies about formation mechanism of C-dots from small molecules, we assume that formation mechanism is as follows: (1) hydrolysis, dehydration and fragmentation of small molecules; (2) polymerization of the products formed in step.1 and aromatization of the polymers; (3) nucleation and growth of carbon substances formed in previous step; (4) oxidation of grown carbon substances [30].

In the first step, one plausible mechanism could be that sucrose is initially hydrolyzed into glucose and fructose, which can be further dehydrated into furfural intermediates, such as (hydroxyl methyl) furfural [31,32]. Then these intermediates polymerized into polymers and aromatized to aromatic compounds which make the color of solution turn to orange and red [33,34]. In the next step, the solution reached a critical super-saturation and a single burst of nucleation resulted. This step may arise from cross-linking induced by intermolecular dehydration of macromolecules formed in previous step. Then the solute diffused toward the particle surface and nuclei grow uniformly and isotropically until the reaction was terminated [35]. Finally, the grown carbon substances were oxidized to obtain good water solubility [30].

In the absence of sucrose, we found that no C-dots was observed, indicating that the hydrolysis and dehydration of sucrose is key step for the formation of C-dots. We propose that H3PO4 may act as a chemical activation agent to activate sucrose and it may also play an important role in the following steps. There were little C-dots observed by TEM in the presence of low concentrations of H3PO4 (1.5mol L−1), which confirmed the important role of high concentrations of H3PO4 played in the formation of C-dots. Moreover, we have found that the reaction becomes slower when the system was heat under an anaerobic condition. This indicates that the oxygen is another key factor in the formation of C-dots we can not ignore.

Formation mechanism of carbogenic materials from carbohydrates have been extensively investigated previously; nonetheless, the reaction path of C-dots, one kind of carbogenic materials, still remains undefined due to the presence of diverse dehydrated intermediates and the limit of techniques identifying all derivable compounds.

The PL property of C-dots was also dependent on the reaction time. We used green C-dots as indicator to study the influence of reaction time on the PL property (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1, the PL intensity of green C-dots increased with the reaction time, reaching a maximum at 4h and then decreasing.

The size distribution and morphology of both green C-dots and yellow C-dots were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a and b, both C-dots are quasispherical and monodisperse with a narrow size distribution. Accordingly, Fig. 2g and h show the size distribution of C-dots. The statistical diameters of both C-dots are 2.7±0.5nm. The size distribution of two C-dots agrees well with the Gaussian distribution and full width half maximum (FWHM) of the fitted curve are 0.55 and 0.31nm, which further confirms the narrow size distribution of as-prepared C-dots. The crystalline nature of both C-dots was investigated by HRTEM. As shown in Fig 2c–f, two C-dots exhibit high-crystalline graphitic structure, both lattice spacings about 0.34 and 0.21nm were observed which are very close to the (002) interlayer spacing and (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite, respectively [36,37]. This result indicates that the both C-dots consist of a graphite-like core.

The surface functional groups and element states of C-dots were characterized by XPS. Both C-dots show two apparent peaks centered at 285.0 and 532.0eV which can attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively (Fig. S2). Another two peaks at 190.0 and 133.2eV associated with P 2s and P 2p are also observed. Therefore, both C-dots can be temporarily thought as phosphorus functionalized. Fig. 3 a shows the XPS spectrum of C 1s for green C-dots. The spectrum can be fitted into four types of carbon bond, corresponding to sp2 (CC) at 284.6, sp3 (C–C) at 285.6, C–OH at 286.7, as well as CO at 287.9eV. Meanwhile, the C 1s spectrum of yellow C-dots was given in Fig. 3b. It can also be deconvoluted into five surface contents, that is, sp2 (CC) at 284.5, sp3 (C–C) at 285.4, C–OH at 286.3, CO at 287.4 and COO at 289.9eV. It should be noted that the XPS data presented here only reflects the surface components of the C-dots, so the relative contents of the core component cannot be obtained.

We also used FTIR to identify the functional groups of two C-dots (Fig. 3c). The FTIR of two C-dots are similar to each other. The existence of a broad peak centered at 3420cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of O–H. A sharp absorption peak at 1630cm−1 associated with CO stretching is observed. Several peaks at 2921, 2854 and 1380cm−1 reveal the presence of CH2 and CH3 on the C-dots. In addition, the spectra display a broad band in the spectral region between 1300 and 900cm−1 with two peaks at 1162 and 1065cm−1. Absorption in this region is usually found in oxidized carbons and has been assigned to C–O stretching. It can also be assigned to phosphorus and phospho-carbonceous exist in phosphoric acid activated carbon [38]. The shoulder peak at 1162cm−1 (Fig. 3c and Fig. S3) can be assigned to the stretching vibration of hydrogen-bonded PO groups from phosphates or polyphosphates, to the O–C stretching vibration of P–O–C(aromatic) [39]. The functional groups identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS.

Both XPS and FTIR reveal that the surfaces of two C-dots are functionalized with phosphoric groups. In order to further characterize the element states of phosphorus of two C-dots, 31P NMR spectra were measured. In the 31P NMR spectra (Fig. 3d), both C-dots exhibit a sharp peak centered at about 2.0ppm indicating the presence of P–O bond on the surfaces rather than P–H due to the peaks observed are not splitting. This is consistent with results of FTIR that PO exists on the surface. In addition, as shown in the high resolution P 2p peak and fitted spectra (Fig. S4), both C-dots show the P 2p peak at about 133.6 and 133.8eV for green C-dots and yellow C-dots, respectively, which allow the identification of the phosphorus species as tetra-coordinated phosphorous (V) [40]. The phosphate, [PO4 3−], is modified on the surface of graphite core of C-dots by bidentate bonding, with C–OPO (OH)2 as surface ends [41]. The phosphate on the C-dots may be derived from the thermal dehydration of hydroxyl phosphate moieties between –OH on the surface of carbon core and –PO (OH) of the H3PO4.

The optical properties of both C-dots are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4a), both C-dots show two apparent absorption peaks: (1) a peak at 284nm, which is due to the n →π∗ transition of CO bond and (2) a weaker peak at 225nm, which attributed to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 bond [42]. Compared with green C-dots, yellow C-dots exhibit a red-shifted absorption edge. The PL spectra of two C-dots are shown in Fig. 4b, it can be seen that green C-dots show a strong PL at 514nm when they are excited at 420nm while yellow C-dots display an obvious PL at 536nm excited at 420nm. Using 9,10-bis (phenylethynyl) anthracene (QY=1 in cyclohexane) as a reference, the QY of the C-dots in water is determined to be 4% for green C-dots and 3% for yellow C-dots, which is comparable to previous reports [16]. As shown in the insets of Fig. 1, the green C-dots emit green PL and yellow C-dots emit yellow PL under 365nm UV lamp. Interestingly, the emission from green C-dots only shift about only 10nm while the emission from yellow C-dots shifts about 30nm which is larger than that of green C-dots with excitation wavelength (λ ex) increasing from 390 to 460nm (Fig. 4c and d). The λ ex dependent PL behavior is common in C-dots. Exceptionally, a few C-dots have also been reported to emit PL independent of λ ex [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study on the origin of PL
ce:section.ce:para: From the application point, PL is one of the most attractive properties of C-dots. So far, most studies attributed the PL of C-dots to differently sized nanoparticles (quantum effect) and/or surface-defects existed in the C-dots or a mechanism currently unresolved. Kang et al. [10] claimed that the strong PL of C-dots originated from the quantum-sized graphite structure itself rather than the carbon–oxygen surface. The PL may not only come from the particles with a certain size distribution, but also from different emissive sites existed on the surface of C-dots. Accordingly, Du et al. [44] claimed that carboxylate or different oxygen-containing groups formed on the particle were the origin of the PL. In our study, it is very clear that the same size distribution can not cause the λ ex dependent or λ ex independent behavior. So the PL of C-dots may derive from the different surface oxidation degrees. This deduction is confirmed by XPS. The XPS show that the content of oxygen-containing groups (C–OH, CO and COO) formed on the yellow C-dots is greater than that of green C-dots (Fig. S5 and Table S1). Our results are quite similar to the conclusions put forward by Pang et al. [45]. They proposed that the different PL behavior of shifting and non-shifting C-dots derived from the different surface oxidation degrees. In other words, various oxygen-containing groups introduced onto the surface of C-dots can act as surface states with the energy levels between π and π∗ states of CC and induce absorption bands due to the electron transition within one or several of these groups [43]. However, the actual origin of PL remains unsettled and needs further investigation.

The stability of both C-dots under various conditions has also been studied. The PL intensity of two C-dots under pH from 2 to 13 is measured (Fig. 5 a). It is very interesting to see that the PL intensity of green C-dots keeps constant in the pH range from 7.0 to 11.0, though the PL intensity drops obviously outside this range. In particular, the PL intensity at 420nm excitation increases linearly with the increase of pH from 2.0 to 7.0 (Fig. S6). It indicates that the green C-dots have potential as fluorescent sensors for pH measurements. Compared with green C-dots, the PL intensity of yellow C-dots is pH independent over a wider range (5.0–12.0) and the PL intensity decreases slightly (about 25% at pH=2.0 and 20% at pH=13.0) with the pH increase or decrease. The different pH dependent behavior of these two C-dots may originate from different content of oxygen containing groups introduced on their surface. What’s more, there is no change in PL intensity for two C-dots at different concentrations of KCl (Fig. 5b), which is very important because it is necessary for C-dots to be used in the presence of physical salt concentrations in practical applications. In addition, we also study the influence of various external cations on the PL intensity of both C-dots. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL intensity of both C-dots remains constant even the concentrations of cations reach up to 30μmol L−1 except that Cu2+ decrease the PL of yellow C-dots a little. Additionally, the PL intensity of both C-dots does not change even exposed to UV excitation for hours which reveal the good photo stability of C-dots possess (Fig. 5d). This excellent tolerance to pH in a wide range, high ionic strength, external cations and UV excitation makes the as-prepared C-dots have great potential in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and application in cell imaging of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Before applying C-dots as bioimaging agents, we studied their stability in physiological conditions at first. We observed that both C-dots did not form any sediment after being incubated in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), FBS or saline for 72h (Fig. S7), which indicated that both C-dots are quite stable in physiological conditions [46]. The viability of HeLa, a human cervical cancer cell line and GES-1, a human gastric normal cell line were examined when cells were exposed to 0–300μg mL−1 green C-dots or yellow C-dots for 48h. There is not any obvious reduction of the cell viability for both cell lines when exposed to high concentrations (up to 300μg mL−1) of C-dots (Fig. 6 ). This result indicates the as-prepared C-dots are biocompatiable and have great potential to replace the conventional semiconductor QDs to be used in biological field, e.g., bioimaging or bioanalysis etc.

We use HeLa cells as example to explore the potential of C-dots as a bioimaging agent. HeLa cells were incubated with green C-dots or yellow C-dots for 18h to demonstrate their possibility as bioimaging agents. Cellular uptake of C-dots was observed by confocal microscopy. As shown in the bright field image of HeLa cells incubated with green C-dots (Fig. 7 a) or yellow C-dots (Fig. 7d), the normal morphology of cells further verify that the as-prepared C-dots are biocompatible and possess minimum toxicity effects towards bioimaging. In addition, it is clear to see that cells show green PL for green C-dots (Fig. 7b) or bright yellow PL for yellow C-dots (Fig. 7e) excited at 405nm after incubation with C-dots. Merged images (Fig. 7c and f) further demonstrate the ability of both C-dots to penetrate into the cell membrane without any further surface passivation. This observation provides promising candidates for bioimaging agents for living cells.

Moreover, we have also performed another experiment to study that whether two C-dots can be taken up by HeLa cells together or not. So we incubated cells with green C-dots and yellow C-dots to perform the bioimaging experiments. As shown in the Fig. S8, both green and yellow PL spots can be observed inside the cells. This may indicates that two C-dots can be taken up by HeLa cells together.

Phosphate functionalized and two color C-dots have been prepared through one step acidic oxidation of sucrose by H3PO4 at low temperature (60°C). The as-prepared C-dots are monodisperse with narrow size distribution and show satisfactory fluorescence QY (4% for green C-dots and 3% for yellow C-dots). In addition, both C-dots display excellent stability towards various conditions, including pH in a wide range (2.0–13.0), high ionic strength, UV excitation and external cations. Both C-dots can be taken up by cells without any further surface passivation. The as-prepared C-dots have great potential in bioimaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Chinese 973 Program (Grant No. 2011CB933600), National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 21225313), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21203035), Large-scale Instrument And Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (2012203020203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.09.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications

Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Solution phase synthesis of carbon quantum dots as sensitizers for nanocrystalline TiO2 solar cells
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon quantum dots/Cu2O composites with protruding nanostructures and their highly efficient (near) infrared photocatalytic behavior
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots

Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Observation of pH-, solvent-, spin-, and excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence from carbon nanoparticles

Microwave–hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from graphite oxide
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Transforming C60 molecules into graphene quantum dots
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Luminescent hollow carbon shells and fullerene-like carbon spheres produced by laser ablation with toluene
New strategy for synthesis and functionalization of carbon nanoparticles
Sulfur-incorporated carbon quantum dots with a strong long-wavelength absorption band
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Synthesis, functionalization and bioimaging applications of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
Bioinspired growth of crystalline carbonate apatite on biodegradable polymer substrata
Highly stable performance of supercapacitors from phosphorus-enriched carbons
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: electrochemical synthesis and their pH sensitive photoluminescence properties
Hydrothermal preparation of carbon microspheres from mono-saccharides and phenolic compounds
Formation of highly luminescent nearly monodisperse carbon quantum dots via emulsion-templated carbonization of carbohydrates
Reaction model of cellulose decomposition in near-critical water and fermentation of products
Large scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots and their application for bioimaging
Colloidal carbon spheres and their core/shell structures with noble-metal nanoparticles
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Modification of the surfaces of a gasactivated carbon and a chemically activated carbon with nitric acid, hypochlorite, and ammonia
Oxygen and phosphorus enriched carbons from lignocellulosic material
Phosphate-functionalized carbon monoliths from deep eutectic solvents and their use as monolithic electrodes in supercapacitors
Enhanced visible photocatalytic activity of nanocrystalline α-Fe2O3 by coupling phosphate-functionalized graphene
High yield preparation of macroscopic graphene oxide membranes
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Shifting and non-shifting fluorescence emitted by carbon nanodots
In vivo NIR fluorescence imaging, biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
